commentBody: Easy breezy Sunday.  Cute theme, well below my average solve time for a Sunday.

This was a well constructed grid, not difficult but not boring, with quality fill and some nice stacks on the sides. I enjoyed this cute theme. My only nit is CUETIPS. Everything else is a straightforward entry but CUETIPS sounds like a pun on QTIPS. I assume as Caitlin suggests this means the ends of pool cues—but to me this isn’t exactly a common phrase and seems a bit green-painty. But the others are very legit and I liked the first and last especially because they combined letters. Maybe a few more clever or misdirecting clues would have made me enjoy this even more but this was a good time overall.

Personal best for a Sunday at 18 minutes. But enjoyable if easy-breezy. I'm noticing a lot of people are complaining that the puzzles are getting two mainstream-easy but I also noticed that a lot of people complain when they are too hard. Maybe there's no pleasing everyone? Either way, enjoyable puzzle and I can get my fix in the archive if I need to spend more time staring at my phone. Thanks for a fun one!
I've probably never heard/seen the term Altiplano before today. Now I've seen it twice!

[Presumptuous ones]
WHI(P)(P)ERSNA(P)(P)ERS



[The circles in the above answer?]
SPLIT PEAS

I finished in about half my typical Sunday time. I worked from bottom up and rarely had to pause before filling in the answers. It took a bit to figure out the trick for the companions to the circled entries, but the crosses helped. An enjoyable diversion.

@Caitlin. At least in the Roman tradition, technically,  the MISSAL provides the rubric for the celebrant of  those parts of the Mass that are common, while the readings are contained in a lectionary.
 However, the book combining both provided for the congregation is also styled MISSAL.

I like playing the triangle. It's acute instrument.

(Sorry for the tangent.)

Am the only one who has never heard of “duded up”? I read a lot and am always confused when I find a phrase that is completely unknown to me. Usually I end up thinking “oh yeah I’ve seen/heard that before!”, but not this time.  Who uses that phrase and in what time period?

Puzzle: Are you with me?

Me: I gotta be honest here -- not until the very end.

Nicely done, Alex Eaton-Salners!

When I see Alex Eaton-Salners is the constructor, I have a high MOTIVATION to jump into solving right away. He is the EMBODIMENT of inspiration, puzzlement, and fun.

(go read AES notes if you wonder what I’m on about.)

Very clever and fun,  I enjoyed the cryptic-like nature of the theme. 

SITTING PRETTY / TEA SETS is my favorite pair.

What do DAISY, YAREN, ANTIMONY and EGYPT have in common?

CROSSWISE

I’m still new to crosswords so I was proud of myself when I got past halfway before turning on Autocheck; usually Sundays I barely even get started before resorting to Autocheck for help!

I really enjoyed today’s theme, there were so many fun clues. I originally had secretCITY but quickly realised my mistake from the crossing words, and then I nearly worked out the letter puzzle on that one all by myself (I first tried iseeEYETOEYE, which threw me right off for a while).

My favourite letter puzzle was TEASETS, and on a related note I enjoyed today’s puzzle with a cup of French Earl Grey tea; a lovely way to spend a Sunday morning.

A nitlet about YAREN and its country Nauru.  Nauru is tiny.  Four Naurus could fit inside Manhattan, which itself is tiny, only 12 blocks wide at its widest.  It does not have cities.  Its entire population is smaller than one of Manhattan's zip codes.  It has districts, which have populations in the hundreds.  YAREN is one of those districts.  It is not a city, and is not designated as the capital.  Its population is smaller than that of many residential buildings in Manhattan.  It's where the seat of government is, but technically, there's no capital in Nauru.  YAREN is considered, though, to be the "de facto" capital because a country has to have one, doesn't it?

(Not saying the puzzle is wrong...)

What an excellent puzzle. 

Astounding  creativity and on point execution.
 
It was great fun to figure out the themers, and the fill was so  engaging as to never feel like a slog.

Thank you much, Mr. Eaton-Salners!

Some data that can be used to begin to understand the relative difficulty of puzzles “now” and “back then”.    

In early COVID days, I did an extensive analysis of my personal solve times across a 1600+ streak that was broken in October 2019.  My average weekly sum of solve times, Sunday to Saturday, was x minutes. I obviously had way too much time on my locked down hands.    

Using similar methodology my weekly solve times across my current 700+ streak is 0.98x.   

Good samples sizes, similar solving conditions, highly similar results. Using the weekly average suppressed a lot of variability, but leveled the playing field.   

I’m not gonna do a chi squared or t test to refine the results ( my Minitab subscription lapsed years ago) but from my viewpoint, over the long game, puzzles today are of similar difficulty to puzzles a decade ago.     

My results could be argued either way.
Fun fact: Nauru is the only country in the world with no official capital city. Yaren district is more of a de facto administrative centre.
This was fun! Loved the theme. Loved lots of the other fill. Especially LEIS for [island chains]. I hope y’all have a great Sunday. Thanks, Alex!

Fun puzzle today but a bit proper noun heavy for my taste.  Terra Haute, Ind is probably as well known nationally as Natick, MA.  (I happen to live near Natick, MA.)

Theme was fine but nothing exciting, just kind of obvious letter spelling jokes.

But obscure references like LOTTE Lenya, and weird obscure words like DUDED, it felt less like I was figuring out clever clues and more like I was guessing phonetically and hoping it might be right.
I'm still waiting for someone at NYT to acknowledge the issues with accessing the midi puzzle from Australia. Anyone??
I had ‘forties’ for 91 across (the circles in 93 across), until none of the cross words worked.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Finally I know what day of the week it is.
Cmon "yaren" could anything be more obscure?? Other than that nice puzzle

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Can someone kindy explain 117A to me?  

I got it only by crosses.
LOVED this puzzle. Great fun with circled letter play. Was very happy that letter play only included actual words. Everything made sense. Well done on this construction!

First time in a few years for a new personal best. I just kept typing. It did feel easy. One thing I've done in the last month or so was to go back to doing Fridays and Saturdays, which I suspect makes the other days less intimidating (most days are getting faster).

A good Sunday morning puzzle. 

Thanks.

It does not matter how easy or hard a Sunday grid is judged by the masses. I will never be able to do one quickly. They mentally defeat me from the get go, and I usually don’t try them. They are just too hard to do in my phone.  15x15 is perfect for my ‘old’ non Max iPhone. 21x21 is an abomination. I know I can zoom in, but somehow, that makes it harder for my brain. 

Even when I do them, I unwittingly self sabotage. I did both Friday and Saturday sun 15 minutes. Sunday, it took 50 minutes and just a blue star of shame. After countless passes of looking for my error, I finally gave up and hit check puzzle. My mistake?
I don’t know how to spell EygPT. 

comedian first name.  TIy Nataro. “Sounds like a reasonable ethnic name to me. I’ll YouTube them later to see if they are funny”
The element Sb.  ANTIMONg. “Sounds weird, but okay. Science be science”

Sunday. You win. You always win…
fun!
Another great puzzle! Thanks for the challenge.
Why do we have a Thursday puzzle on a Sunday

What happened to the Easy Version of Fridays Crossword Puzzle???
You no longer offer them!!!
Great Sunday puzzle. Very clever theme entries that were fun to figure out.Tea sets was my first and favorite solve.

Yeah for QBERT! 
Used to play this on an Atari plugged into my sister's 14" b/w TV.
Good times...
What a fun and very cool puzzle. Loved the moment when it clicked—

Here’s to YAREN.  It is not a city or even an official capital, as its country, Nauru, has neither, according to Wikipedia.  Yaren’s population (2021) is 803, a mighty climb from its 2011 population of 747.  

That is, Yaren is a little ‘un as capitals and cities go.  A speck, hardly noticed outside of itself.  And yet here it is in the prestigious Times crossword, sharing the box with the likes of KATE Winslett, Lisa LOEB, and ETTA James.  And not even Bad Bunny has appeared in a Times crossword.

You go YAREN!  You are not only literally on the map, but tiny you out in who-knows-where, are now On The Map.  Cheers to you!

Well, these letter-sound puns looked to me like they’d be easy to come up with – until I tried, and for me, at least, it was tough going.

Alex not only came up with five of them, but made them symmetrical in the grid.  Thus, INSTRUMENT / ARE YOU WITH ME – 10 letters and 12 letters – is matched below by SEES EYE TO EYE / ATOMIC CITY at 12 letters and 10 letters.  And so on.

So, not only are the theme answers tough to come up with, IMO, but the possibilities are greatly constricted by the demands of symmetry.

Wow and bravo to you on coming up with this theme answer set, Alex.

I especially liked the sees-eye-to-eye / ICCI conceit.  My favorite clue in the box?  That batty [Batteries for brains?] for IQ tests.  It was also sweet seeing ATTA beneath ETTA.

All of this is to say that I was impressed and entertained by your creation, Alex – that it was time very well spent.  Thank you!
Here's Bobby Darin singing about Lotte Lenya: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yYZY9P4O4cQ Lotte Lenya performed in an off-Broadway production of The Threepenny Opera in New York in the 1950s.

Funny/cool that 56 and 93 Across clues both show up the morning after my daughter closed her high school musical production of Annie!

Also good puzzle.
OTOMI? Who knew? Here are some nice views of the embroidery they are known for: https://www.otomimexico.com/single-post/otomi-embroidery https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nMsbr02DGcY

Loved that the descriptor themers
did not give us an alternate clue.

(Like adding,
…or “So we agree?” 
to the clue for
ARE YOU WITH ME.)

Bit more crunch.

And we all need our fiber.
I've read all the comments to date and I'm stunned that no one has commented on MAFIOSOS. The plural of Mafioso is MAFIOSI, and M-W backs this up. You don't have to speak Italian to know this.
FETLOCKS is just completely wrong, given FEATHERS would be the correct answer and fits the space. Not my favourite clue if I'm being pedantic
Delightful.

A fine Sunday puzzle, with a nice theme and good level of difficulty.
Nice to see my old friend QBERT in the puzzle.

In the TIL file:  OTOMI and YAREN, both of which had to be inferred solely from the crosses.  The N of YAREN was my last letter to fall.

Fun fact:  Not only is every little tuft of a dandelion a SEEDLET, but every little "petal" is a little flower, a "floret".  If you pull one out of the head, you can see its little stamens and stigma and ovary.  (This is true of a DAISY, as well.)

Nit du jour:  Three SETS makes for a nice tennis match, but is two too many for a crossword (IMHO).

[Each of the tiny tufts of a dandelion has one]
(SEE(D)(L)ET)

[The brackets in that answer?]
WHAT DE ELL???!!

“Seedlet” is nearly as rare since 1900 at Google ngrams as “aseethe”. A poetic term, not botanical.

Still, a fun puzzle.

Etta James was great...but ENOUGH ALREADY!
This puzzle made up for that with the marvelous "Are you with me?" clue.
The one tough square for me was the X in "SXSW" and "UBERX." I was so sure it was a "B" (South BY Southwest -- hello??), and UBERB seemed reasonable as the lower tier of that service (UBERA would be the upper one). Alas, no.

New game, what do you think:

Spot the clues that have been edited to be easier.

Submit your list to the chat.

I'll go first:

84A "rowboat blades"

Clever theme and a good long workout for me. Nice moments as each trick finally dawned on me. And.. a perhaps appropriate puzzle find today - a Sunday from September 28, 1969 by Edward J. O'Brien with the title: "Rhymes from way out." Don't recall seeing another quite like this. A few of the theme answers: PAYDAYWAYLAYNAYSAY WHITECOLLARDOLLARHOLLER HEMIDEMISEMIQUAVERSAVER ALONGSIDETOGUIDETHERIDE SOMEFEWWILLVIEWWHOLLBOO ACOBBLEJOBLLWOBBLE ANYMOREMINIMODESIMMORAL ADIREFIREWIRE And there were more. Don't think I would have had much of a chance at that one. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/28/1969&g=24&d=A ...

I have another idea for some CUE TIPS:

Try  it to stare at the cards when you read your lines.
Make sure your scene partner is done with their lines before you start yours.

This one defeated me, but that was entirely my fault. Can't blame the puzzle, which struck me as being especially good. 

Entered SOTHATISIT instead of SOTHISISIT, and failed to check the crosses before filling my last box. Didn't know MISSAL, but would have seen that my LEAS should have been LEIS! So, another streak ended ... though it happens frequently enough that I don't pay (much) attention anymore. 

Liked the clever theme, and enjoyed some of the interesting entries, especially ANTIMONY and FETLOCKS. The LOTTO/MOTTO and ETTA/ATTA pairings were cute as well. I also liked seeing ILIED and LYING, with one about telling untruths and the other about reclining.

Yes, in Italian it should be MAFIOSI, but MAFIOSOS is acceptable in English. Just anticipating complaints about that one. 

A very enjoyable and well-crafted Sunday puzzle ... even though it made me stumble.

I learned about YAREN today, but I've heard about Nauru before.
It's worth spending a few minutes of your time to learn about the sad history of the island "nation" of Nauru in the 20th century.

The island was essentially a large pile of guano (bird poo) formed by passing seabirds over many millennia.  At the beginning of the century, it was hollowed out for its rich fertilizer and turned into a desert, no longer of interest to most living creatures.
At the end of the century, the Australians were using it as a "processing" holding area for asylum seekers.  The stark conditions were intended to discourage all but the most serious "seekers".

Let's hope that it's prospects improve in the 21st century
Encore!
I am repeatedly awed by the cleverness and creativity of themed puzzles. How do people come up with this stuff? Bravo, Alex!

Clever Sunday puzzle.
70D is making one heck of an assumption.

The Midi is a little odd.

The Mini is low-hanging fruit.

I’d be willing to bet I’m the only person who failed this because of qUETIPS/qDR. I just assumed it was an American brand plus I know almost nothing about military ranks, especially abbreviated military ranks.

Alex Eaton-Salners is always welcome at my breakfast table...I just wish he wouldn't let my coffee get cold.  (When the answers/entries are literally streaming, one doesn't want to stop for a swig of java...)

I had the worst time recalling the Spanish vocabulary word, which I am sure came up very, very early in 8th grade Spanish I....
I was out of sync with Alex...wanted CLUB DUES at 26A and ADAGE at 106D plus TALE for 109D....  Gave the FriXion eraser quite the workout.  

39A... Ah, memories!...The post chapel at Schofield Barracks held services for all religious groups (though Protestants were lumped together) and thus was somewhat unadorned, and there were various hymnals and other books shelved on the backs of the pews.  I entertained myself during sermons by reading the unfamiliar ones....which included "My Sunday MISSAL"
Fastest Sunday yet. But I've only been solving since Xmas.

Steal My Sunshine, intro: Hey, Matt Yeah, Tim? Hey, you talked to Marc lately? Uh, haven't really talked to him but he looks pretty, uh, down "He looks pretty, uh, down?" Yeah, well, maybe we should cheer him up then What do you, uh, suppose we should do? Well, does he like butter tarts? Canadian, eh? love those butter tarts. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=teUlISh-TiA
Enjoyed this puzzle a lot! Had troubles with STASES as states, stairs, and static all fit. Took until I got I DIE to finish!
Why don't crossword writers understand that the degree for a CFO is MBE (not MBA)?  MBA is the degree for the CEO, not the CFO.
So is a commander like a helmsman? Too lazy to scroll down 😎 Fun puzzle, q-tips almost got me

What a great Sunday offering; crunchy, chewy and very satisfying. Not too difficult but certainly with a few chewy areas. I spent quite some time fly specking for my error. Finally found it; don’t ask me why but I’d put crypt for EGYPT.  
Not going to argue with LLAMAS instead of Alpacas. I guess their larger brethren should get a look in occasionally, just don’t get in spitting range. My poor girls have to have a TB test as it’s been found in the herd they came from. Not at all concerned for them as it’s been over 12 months since they left, but officialdom demands. Naturally I have to pay the vet bill.

Y'see, this is the kind of creativity that hacks like me can aspire to but will almost certainly never achieve. After working a puzzle like this, I realize the best I could possibly pull off might be a Monday or Tuesday level puzzle. No shade on those constructors – their works are just simpler by nature. I'll keep trying, but don't nobody hold your breath 😐
Nice and crunchy. best of the week! Theme was *chef's kiss*
LOVED IT!  Great Sunday puzzle. Thanks, Alex Eaton-Salners!
That was a very clever puzzle Alex, and very enjoyable also.  Thank you.

I saw eye to eye with the constructor on this delightful Sunday offering.  Exactly what a Sunday puzzle was meant to be.  Once I caught on, I actually used the themed answers to help solve puzzle.  Pure joy to start spring.

Note to self: EROTIC CITY is the Prince song, not the Manhattan Project site. Fine puzzle, amusing theme. I can see why it took the constructor a year to make it work. Nota bene: I figured out BEELINE before the corresponding entry, and dropped in the Bs accordingly.

If the easy easy puzzle trend continues, I’ll cancel my subscription. It’s been a great run of crosswords, but this isn’t enjoyable. I am aware of all the folks who love and support the easy trend. Peace be with you.

That was a challenge, but enjoyed working my my through it.  Took a while, as I had entries like ATOMICCAFE, SOTHATISIT, WESEEEYETOEYE (and others) to steer me off course.  Had not heard of QUIETQUITING or FETLOCKS and my Army experience had me insisting it was some version of LTl (first lieutenant) and not the Naval CDR.  Well constructed.

Alex Eaton-Salners is on my top ten list of constructors; he never disappoints. This puzzle was entertaining, the theme was the perfect level of clever, and I enjoyed the mental gymnastics of the themed entries. This was truly a perfect Sunday puzzle for me.

[Question at Hispanic (silent-end) honorary group?] W(H)OST(H)EW(H)OLEENC(H)ILADA [The circles in xx across?] THEFOURHCLUB I know it's silly, but do I have to explain it? Hs are not pronounced in Spanish, I don't think! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=slBYkmqn7qs
I really liked this. I found it very soothing for a migraine headache. Checked puzzle in the end and had two mistakes and two typos at least.  Very clever!  Thank you.

The app marks my puzzle as almost completed, but checking the answer key shows all correct entries. Anyone else having a similar issue? I don’t want to auto check the puzzle because that will break my streak. Of course, it’s feasible that I have a typo, but I’ve checked three times and can’t find one. Great puzzle, despite technical (or possibly, user) error!
This was a fun diversion that taught me a few new words; I'd call that pretty good for a Sunday

What the heck? It was easier to solve the puzzle then to grasp what the constructor is talking about. Huh? Motivations and embodiments? What field of knowledge is that? Sounds like some kind of mystical psychiatry to me. O, btw, columnist, the Qs were not at the ends of the words; they were at the beginnings of the words. Still it was fun to solve. Almost got it all right except for 3 words.

Very clever puzzle, full of fun clues. I kept thinking I had it, and then had to go back and change a few letters. I always like a call out to the Midwest (Terre Haute). I have no recollection of Qbert, but I'll take your word for it. 1980's video games? I thought all we had was Tetris, Pong and Frogger back then.

I enjoyed that one. For me, it required deciphering the theme clues to finish. Having the three middle themers be repeated letters was a nice misdirect for sussing out the top and bottom ones. I never had a moment of being stopped in my tracks, but having to rethink the theme lifted this puzzle above the predictable. The whole picture didn't become clear until the very end and that was a pleasant change. Fittingly enough, my last fill was a Z.

"Missal" is a word that is somewhat triggering for me.

My grandfather was a very loving man, and I loved him very much. But he was, as was common in the time, a man of quite pronounced prejudices.  One of them was anti-clericalism, as he was evangelical.

One day, when I was probably five or so, he was showing me how the Catholic Bible wasn't really a Bible, but just a lot of "missal". 

Strange how vivid that memory remains.

It hurts to think about my Grandpa as a religious bigοt, but I try to regard him as a product of the times and of the education that was available to him .  

Wheels within wheels, tribes within tribes.

Two fabulous Sunday puzzles in a row! I loved this clever theme, and it felt breezy enough so as to not be a slog, but still chock full of TILs and crunchy clueing. I'm not sure if it was intentional, but I smiled at the bonus BESETS crossing BABYBIBS.

Saved this to enjoy in the afternoon, and it didn't disappoint. Fun theme. About half average time. 
Thought it was going to rain and give me an excuse not to walk today, but no. Got out for my 5 miles. Granddaughter sang at two Masses today (yes, I easily got the "MISSAL" clue!), and what a joy to be able to watch online. 
Have a good day. Go, Red Storm!
Re: QUIET QUITTING - I'm still vaguely annoyed that they felt compelled to come up with a hip new term for "not doing unpaid work for your employer".

Agonizingly difficult - too many times I had to resort to WordTips.

BTW, "niter" is a red herring (clue: Component of fertilizer and gunpowder), inasmuch as it contains just some of the letters of the correct answer "nitrate".

I suppose you have to  accommodate the puzzle constructor's idioms.

/rant over

Sometimes I have a lot to say, as you may have noticed....  but today I just want to say that this was a very nice puzzle and I enjoyed it a lot!  Really enjoyed all the theme pairs!  Fun! Terrific Sunday puzzle!! ☺️

One more puzzle - one of the oddest I've ever encountered. A Wednesday from January 6, 2010 by Julian Lim. Theme clues and answers: "Race energetically past?" FLYSPRYLYBY Native African's musical beat?" PYGMYRHYTHM "Where an old wanderer is interred?" GYPSYCRYPT "What Romeo and Juliet had to do?" SYNCTRYSTS And the 'reveal': "Like King Solomon... or an oral hint to 17 and 62 across and 11 and 29 down." ALLWISE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/6/2010&g=17&d=A ...

How does one find the theme of the puzzle in the app apart from opening hints and tips? Sometimes it shows me but today it just says “the crossword”. 

PS- very fun solve!

Spent a lot of time on 71A, 72D, 81D, 87A, and 88D. Felt great to finally get it even though it involved some guessing. That’s why I never ever look anything up. Amazingly, I was able to work it down to a few letters to guess and finally got lucky. 

After remembering tete was French for head, I then thought of Spanish words for day. All I had was manana so I tried both Mira and Nira for 88D. I had thought Kira, but that didn’t work. For the first letter of 87A I thought of the Spanish word for seven. Wasn’t quite sure but I thought it started with S. That made LOE? and ?ESTBOY candidates for 71A and 72D. A few guesses later got my gold star. 

Luckily I had scanned the puzzle earlier and replaced SBSW for South By Southwest with SXSW. 

Most of the rest of the puzzle was fairly easy. 

Overall, I enjoyed it a lot.

This is the kind of puzzle that makes me nostalgic for my salad days as a naive, carefree twenty three year old Prince of Monaco.  Puttering about on the French Riviera without a clue as to what I would do with my life.  Living day to day, a rather bohemian existence, hobnobbing with royalty and dignitaries, gala events, global summits, exploring the arts and world culture through my political influence and bottomless bank account. Now, as a middle aged man with a stable, if not lucrative vinyl siding business in Kenosha I can exhale a sigh of relief knowing everything eventually worked out, but back then the future was little more than a daunting abyss of uncertainty and crippling self doubt.
TERRE Haute gets into crosswords fairly often, but I was surprised to see Mike BRAUN as an answer. Not a well-known governor.

Did not know semana or yaren, but got by crosses.  Takes me awhile since I don't look anything up.  what got me was the x in uberx crossed with sxsw since I knew neither.  This was the problem when I checked the puzzle at the end.  Just had to guess after that for the right letter.  I had keys for island chains for awhile until I saw it.  Fun theme.
This puzzle was rad. I really enjoyed figuring out the theme, and lots of the individual clues were very fun too.

@John
I would have had such a solve time, but I had to find an error.  Alas, I had the 'denial' for St Petersburg instead of Dresden!  Other than that, a very fun puzzle!
Very little resistance was encountered in solving this puzzle.

@SP. For some reason my Google feed is offering me a lot of snooker. So I am seeing a lot of CUE TIPS being chalked. 
It appears that pool, billiards, snookers are not totally obscure games

@SP

What may be lost on you is that Q-Tips is a play on CUE TIPS, not the other way around.
@SP "green-painty"????

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight  Green paint is a term crossword enthusiasts use for something that doesn’t feel conversational enough to be a valid entry. Yes, green paint exists, but it isn’t something particular that comes up in conversation. On the other hand, yellow pages were a thing and I guess they still are in some form.

@Steve L

That was lost on me. The image of sticking a pool cue in my ear makes me wonder, if that’s the case, what kind of marketer thought that up! I’m not trying to make a federal case about it—clearly pool CUETIPS are a thing—it just struck me as less elegant and more forced that the rest.

@Christine Sites like xwstats provide the data, which the Times won’t for some reason, so we know they’re getting easier. It’s not an impression.

At the end of this week of easier puzzles, for example, we have this Sunday where 72% of users of that site report faster than average times.

@Christine

“I'm noticing a lot of people are complaining that the puzzles are getting two mainstream-easy but I also noticed that a lot of people complain when they are too hard. Maybe there's no pleasing everyone?”

a) When people complain about the puzzles being too hard, they almost never have the self-honesty to come right out and say as much — rather, they complain about the puzzle being “a slog”, “full of trivia”, or “too obscure”, and many accuse constructors of trying to “show off how smart they are”.

b) I honestly don’t see why it should be hard to please *almost* everyone, but it requires people to accept a compromise: for experienced solvers, most of the puzzles in a given week offer very little resistance, and for newer solvers, a couple/few puzzles each week may be too hard to solve without assistance.

Seems to me that formula was working fine for a couple decades…

@Dave K.
The tune "El Cóndor Pasa" was originally written as an orchestral piece by a Peruvian composer. It was made famous by Simon and Garfunkel, with English lyrics by Paul Simon. It's a perfect example of Altiplano music, which I have loved and followed since my folk music days.

commentBody:
@LarryF
Beautiful catch!
@Anita -- Hah!  Good one!

@Nancy

Yes be proud, be very proud!

Congrats on pushing yourself before resorting to autocheck.
That's the way to do it, and pretty soon you'll be able to take the training wheels off and even ride hands-free! 
Practice makes perfect. ;-)

@Nancy

Well done!

The past few weeks of this forum has been filled with posts about solving and the state of our crossword puzzle union. Glad you seem happy with your grid work. Have you tried Lady Grey? It's just as nice - bergamot, yum.

Autocheck? All's fair to help enjoy your solving, in my book. 😉

@Steve L

It's worthwhile to take a deeper dive into the island's history.

Here's what the BBC says:
"In the 21st Century it has been a key part of Australia's controversial offshore detention centre policy. There have been questions surrounding the Australian-run asylum-seeker detention camp, with allegations of human rights abuses and overcrowding.
"Nauru is about 3,000km north-east of Australia. The island's main phosphate mines closed in the 1980s, though phosphate mining and exports resumed in 2005.
"The government estimates that secondary deposits have a remaining life of about 30 years, but the island's landscape has been devastated by over a century of phosphate strip mining."

@Brian, Wordle has a new badge for a “Sea of Greens.” You earn it each time you solve a Wordle with all green letters with no yellows.

My XW Stats calendar these last few months has been a Sea of Greens: Almost all puzzles have suddenly been >20% faster than my average solve time for those days. The stats show that is true for most solvers.

@Brian
If you do any data analysis, I would recommend the statistical programming language R. It will do anything statistical and it will do it in style. There's a learning curve. But it's widely used and free (open source).

@Weak
Just Print on Two Pages...  The clues are all on one page, the large grid is on the other sheet, and there are generous margins for guesses or other notes (like, "put BACON on shopping list") 

A FriXion pen (erasable) is also handy.  
When you print the next puzzles on the backs of those two pages, the fill (and notes) will disappear due to the heat.  
Then you recycle the paper.
Voila!

We will likely be dandelion- and even coltsfoot-free for another month at least.
Now that I reconsider how late we are in March, I will revise about the coltsfoot - they could bloom any time now, on open, thawed ground. But dandelions, long time yet.

commentBody:
@Down_Home
Yes, keep trying. 
After you've worked on a puzzle for five hours (or 15), taking down and rebuilding that troll-like southeast block for the third time, you are the least qualified to judge its freshness or crunchiness. 
But it may seem wondrous to the innocent player who hasn't seen the stitching, repairs, and duct tape.
@Grant LOL
@Elaine It’s a little sad to think that the caliber of NYT puzzles we used to enjoy may be a thing of the past.

@Elaine, this puzzle was amusing, entertaining, and the theme required fun mental gymnastics. What else, exactly, are you looking for on a Sunday? Sunday isn't known for difficulty. 

I've been solving for decades; I'm quite good at it. I do see a trend of "easier" puzzles, though not to an extent that I will give them up. It's a daily brain workout that I look forward to completing at the end of my workday. Overall, I find Friday and Saturday challenging enough; though they're sometimes notably easier, I don't feel they've descended to the realm of easy. But that's just one woman's opinion, of course.

@Susan E I can see my average and best times over the years they track it. Not only have the puzzles “felt” easier, they are verifiably so. It is great that you enjoy the easy puzzles. The puzzles will continue with a robust audience.

Elaine --
While I decry the easy trend, 
I'd call this a Sunday of average difficulty.

@Elaine
XWStats is showing this one to be of average difficulty.

It seemed average to me, but I didn't trust that perception because some rated Easy I find very difficult and vice versa. 

I agree with you in wanting stiff puzzles on Thursday - Saturday, and one on Sunday challenging enough to create a diversion from, well, everything. Sorry this one did not do that for you.

@Elaine

Don't let the door hit you on the way out

NYC Traveler,
Click on the link in the reply above your reply.

@Francis
I can relate to this.

My dad was a minister who was very anti-anyone who was not open-minded. In other words, he was scathingly close-minded about being close-minded.

Took awhile for us kids to figure that out.

@Francis
There's more to that story, clearly. For another time.

@Francis ETAL

I can certainly relate to a lot of this. As I've said out here a few times, I am a Christian who fell far from the family tree. Much to their dismay....  And don't get me started on the religious right. I mean I guess I already got started on it since my family is part of it, but it's heartbreaking to me. 

I rewatched the latest Knives Out movie, Wake Up Dead Man, with some friends last night. I'd watched it before and I'd still really highly recommend it. It touches on alot of what we're talking about in this thread. I thought it was beautiful. And I think persons of sincere faith and atheists alike would enjoy it and take something from it. 

It also touches me somewhat personally because of, again, as I've mentioned before, I've been having a mysterious widespread issue of nerve pain throughout my body this last year and a half that there are no answers for, and there's a character in the movie who has the same thing and the themes of hope and faith and lost faith and reenvisioned faith really struck a few chords.

@Lynn

I like to think I honestly don't care what others believe as long as they don't try to force me to believe it as well (like by posting the Ten Commandments in public schools), or structure laws and society based on those religious beliefs (like telling me who I can and cannot enjoy the secular law benefits of marriage.)

I'm not troubled by Islamic clothing, or Jewish side locks or the classic Mormon white shirt and black tie, going door to door.

And as long as I'm not asked to change my beliefs because "God is mad at us for what you believe and will send hurricanes and tornadoes and floods to register their disapproval, and it will all be your fault".

I fell quite far from my grandfather's way of thinking.

@Francis

Beautifully said.

@Tali I was trying to fit “ACT YOUR WAGE” in that spot.

@Disgusted in America

"Niter" is the mineral form. Took me a moment, too.
@Bill Thank you for that because I knew that saltpeter was correct but I didn’t know the chemical name for it. Misspent youth at Valley Forge!

@Disgusted in America - Nitrate would be an inaccurate answer for this clue. NITER is the correct answer. When you are creating a fertilizer, you add potassium nitrate (one option -- see below) as an ingredient -- and NITER is synonymous with potassium nitrate. 

You cannot grab a jar or vat of just "nitrate." It has to be in the salt form -- sodium nitrate, potassium nitrate, ammonium nitrate (careful!), calcium nitrate, and so on. Not simply "nitrate." In fertilizer, the forms of nitrate most commonly used are the potassium or ammonium salts.

@Captain Quahog

Of course, you cannot possess pure "nitrate"; except in perhaps, a customized ionic liquid. Remember balancing valances in HS chemistry (+1 :-1; etc.)?

Potassium nitrate is rarely used in fertilizer. From Google/AI:

Urea is the most common nitrogen-bearing compound used in fertilizer, favored for its high nitrogen content (46% N). Other widely used nitrogen sources include ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, and ammonium phosphate, 

Again, "niter" is completely obscure with regadrds to fertilizer. In my opinion, this is a poorly-worded clue.

@Disgusted in America - I'm glad you have confirmed that you now know your complaint about this clue was completely unfounded. 

Now you need to update your understanding of the use of potassium nitrate in fertilizer. It is one of the most common ingredients, in spite of what your cherry-picked quote says. 

Signed, long-ago technician in the Oregon Ag Extension Service, who worked in... soil fertility. 

P.S. If you are too lazy to do your own "research" let me know and I will be glad to add a whole bunch of citations for you. 

Now, get researchin'!

Just for fun (because I know about this stuff and have no need to consult AI in such matters) I entered the question "do fertilizers contain potassium nitrate" and receive this reply. 

"Yes, many fertilizers contain potassium nitrate (KNO3), which is a highly soluble and efficient source of both potassium (K^+) and nitrogen (NO3) for plants. It is commonly used in high-value, chloride-sensitive crops and in greenhouse/hydroponic, fertilizers because it improves plant health and helps prevent salinity buildup.

Key Details About Potassium Nitrate in Fertilizers: 

• Composition: It provides nutrients in a readily available form, essential for healthy root and shoot development. 
• Chloride-Free: It is favored as a source of potassium that does not contain harmful chlorides. 
• Application: It is used as a stand-alone fertilizer or blended into complex fertilizers, available in powder for liquids or granular (prills) for soil application. 
• Solubility: It dissolves rapidly, making it ideal for fertigation (applying fertilizer through irrigation).

I suggest anyone reading this who thinks I don't know what I'm talking about, despite my extensive background in chemistry and former work in agriculture/soil fertility, go ahead and make a similar query to your favorite AI... 

I also posted a LONG list of URLs about KNO3 used as/in fertilizer, but the EMUs ate it, probably because of all the URLs.

@Rich in Atlanta Oh... and one more puzzle. One with 25 answers that never ever appeared in any other puzzle (and I'm not surprised about that). A couple of theme clues and answers: "Clerical union's demand for a raise." WHITECOLLARDOLLARHOLLER "One who keeps 64th note." HEMIDEMISEMIQUAVERSAVER And a couple of other theme answers: PAYDAYWAYLAYNAYSAY SOMEFEWWILLVIEWWHOLLBOO ALONGSIDETOGUIDETHERIDE AMYMOREMINIMODESIMMORAL Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/28/1969&g=16&d=D ....
